body.text: **Top story:** First real-world GPT-5.4 developer data confirms AI surpasses humans at desktop computer use (75% vs 72.4% human baseline), while Anthropic finalizes legal arguments for what could become the most consequential AI lawsuit in the industry's history.

items.text: **💻 1. GPT-5.4 Real-World Validation: Computer Use Claims Hold Up**

body.text:
24 hours of independent developer testing confirms GPT-5.4's headline computer use capability — and in some cases outperforms OpenAI's published numbers:

• **OSWorld-Verified**: 75.0% (vs 72.4% human baseline) — AI surpasses humans at desktop navigation
• **GDPval knowledge work** (44 occupations): 83.0% vs 70.9% for GPT-5.2
• **SWE-Bench Pro**: 57.7% — edging past GPT-5.3-Codex
• **Property/HOA portal tasks**: 95% success on first attempt (vs ~73–79% with prior CUA models)
• **Tool Search** cutting agentic prompt overhead ~30–50% in early tests
• Model executes via both Playwright code and direct screenshot → mouse/keyboard — two distinct cost/latency profiles
• Some failures on non-standard desktop UIs noted

items.text: **⚖️ 2. Anthropic's Pentagon Legal Strategy Takes Shape**

body.text:
Anthropic's legal team is finalizing its formal complaint against the Pentagon supply-chain risk designation. Three legal pillars:

• **First Amendment** — designation punishes Anthropic for publicly expressing AI safety views (no mass surveillance, no autonomous weapons)
• **Fifth Amendment** — severe penalties imposed without due process or meaningful hearing
• **APA** — Pentagon acted arbitrarily and without statutory authority

The Pentagon insists it cannot allow a private company to restrict AI use in a national security emergency. Industry broadly concerned this precedent could be weaponized against any developer that refuses to remove guardrails. *(Formal suits filed March 9; relief hearing March 24.)*

items.text: **🏛️ 3. AI Legislation Surge: 78 Bills in 27 States; Vermont Signs AI Election Law**

body.text:
Six weeks into the 2026 state legislative session, AI bills are advancing at unprecedented pace:

• **78 chatbot safety bills** alive in **27 states** — broadest multi-state AI push in U.S. history
• **Vermont** signed **S 23** (March 5) — first U.S. law prohibiting AI synthetic media in election campaigns
• **Oregon** gave final approval to chatbot safety bill
• **Washington** — similar measure awaiting governor approval
• **Florida** — Gov. DeSantis's comprehensive AI bill advancing with ~1 week to adjournment
• White House directing DOJ to challenge state AI laws via new **AI Litigation Task Force**; federal preemption framework in development

Without federal action, companies face up to 50 conflicting state AI regulatory regimes.

items.text: **🏢 4. Block's 4,000 Layoffs: AI Transformation or AI-Washing?**

body.text:
Debate intensifies over Jack Dorsey's ~40% workforce cut (4,000 jobs, reducing Block to under 6,000 employees):

• **Dorsey's case**: AI-equipped smaller teams outperform larger ones; restructuring now beats being forced into it reactively; predicts most tech companies follow within a year
• **Skeptics**: Block was already underperforming financially; critics call it "AI-washing" — using AI as cover for standard cost-cutting
• **Block employees** publicly stated AI tools cannot currently perform their specific roles

The episode is forcing boards to pressure-test AI productivity claims. The new boardroom question: "If AI can replace 40% of your workforce, why haven't you restructured?"

items.text: **🎨 5. Supreme Court Settles AI Copyright: Human Authorship Still Required**

body.text:
SCOTUS declined (March 2) to hear Stephen Thaler's appeal seeking copyright for AI-generated artwork by his DABUS system — leaving the human authorship requirement as settled law:

• AI-generated content with no meaningful human creative input **cannot be copyrighted** under current U.S. law
• Lower courts' "human authorship requirement" upheld across the board
• Threshold for what counts as sufficient human creative involvement remains undefined
• Companies using AI-generated outputs for commercial differentiation face IP vulnerability
• Congressional action on AI copyright now looks necessary — but no legislation is currently advancing

• **DeepSeek V4** — Day 6+ since Two Sessions opened; all predicted windows missed. Possible silent "V4 Lite" update spotted on DeepSeek's website, unconfirmed
• **Oregon chatbot safety bill** — Final legislative approval; governor signature expected imminently
• **Samsung** — Confirmed plan to double Gemini AI-equipped devices to **800 million** by end of 2026
• **Florida AI bill** — Advancing with ~1 week to state adjournment; could become largest U.S. state AI law
• **Vermont S 23** — Signed into law; first U.S. prohibition on AI synthetic media in election campaigns

• **DeepSeek V4** — Every passing day raises the stakes; benchmark comparisons vs GPT-5.4 will dominate coverage the moment it drops
• **Anthropic formal lawsuit** — Filing imminent; initial hearing will determine whether Pentagon designation is paused during litigation
• **GPT-5.4 enterprise rollout** — Business/Enterprise Copilot admins enabling access; first large-scale performance reports incoming
• **China Two Sessions** — Continuing through ~March 14; detailed AI funding and AGI pathway documents expected
• **Florida AI bill** — Final vote likely this week
